data.title: AMC Robotics: Related Party Revenue Seems To Be Financing The Business
data.description:
AMC Robotics Corporation (AMCI) is a recently listed SPAC, so there's right away a bit of dilution to consider from options and warrants, though apparently not so much that it seriously caps upside. The bigger issue is the lack of a forward picture since the AI and robotics angle is something that they aren't commercial with yet and are just developing now, so it's very hard to say what cash burn will be in the future, or if there will be cash burn in the future as some of their developing products are supposed to be launched in Q2.

Another thing is that for the existing operations, which are focused on selling security cameras through e-commerce platforms like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), the CEO of AMCI also sits on the other sides of the company's major commercial relationships — that is to say that there are a lot of related party considerations both in revenue and in terms of supply of products for distribution. This makes it even harder to rest assured of the evolution of the financials, since there's no assurance that the CEO will favor AMCI over other companies that AMCI does business with that he also owns. It also seems that its existing security products business is basically being transitioned out, and they will no longer be operating as a distributor of these products quite soon.

So new products will need to support the business, but we aren't sure of the incoming economics of these products. Although, it also seems that a revenue sharing agreement has been put in place to be able to pay AMCI from related party Kami with a revenue share of 30% for as long as those converted subscribers from camera product sales hold. If they stop buying inventory to distribute and then pick up revenue share, cash flows could improve a lot.

Also, they do have $7 million in cash, so they should be able to last another year assuming forward the latest annual cash burn, and perhaps that cash burn is about to shrink to the point where it won't be too much of an issue, though we expect instead mounting R&D, which has been strangely falling, not rising, despite products in the pipeline, and also a new manufacturing hub in Vietnam is likely going to draw on cash too, announced in January. As said, it's unclear how the number will turn in the near future and what the capital needs of the business will be. It is safer to assume that more financing will be needed, and that it's uncertain whether any of the developments will be commercially successful.

Discussion of Current Results

There is a fair bit to discuss. The first is the pretty dramatic change in the sales picture.

Product revenues are down deliberately. They are trying to turnover and distribute products, specifically security cameras, in a higher margin way. Moreover, these sales are supposed to convert customers onto a related party product, a software of a company called Kami also controlled by AMCI's CEO, and share in the revenues from those software sales. There are terms to these sharing arrangements that have improved of late:

Note that the terms have become more generous. That is the CEO deciding to sacrifice flows at Kami and flood them into AMCI. This is good for AMCI shareholders but it also reflects how dependent shareholders are on how these related party moves are decided. In this case, Kami is giving up economics in favor of AMCI.

The associated improvement of economics can be seen above in the significant gross margin lift from greater share of revenue sharing revenues from Kami, the related party.

Forward Considerations

Note that the company is working on Kyro, which is a quadruped robot that is supposed to supplement security cameras by being dispatched to the anomalous location and providing real time surveillance. Then there's the NovaArm, the company's logistics robot. The latter is supposed to become commercial in Q2, the former has a manufacturing base now in Vietnam established after the FY close of books to our understanding, and they did a partnership for GPU compute to enhance their robotics with AI, and this is ultimately where interest in the stock would come from the physicalization of AI for specific purposes. However, despite the developments, R&D expenses are falling. It's the opposite of what you would expect.

While they are gearing up for at least the NovaArm's hopefully profitable release, it seems that selling the security cameras is being sunsetted, or that the economic terms of distributing the cameras might change (as per the 10-K):

entities have authorized the Company to utilize their e-commerce platform accounts free of charge until October 20, 2026. The Authorization Agreements with Xiaoyun and Yishijue will continue until the existing inventory of the Company’s products has been sold, at which time the agreements will be terminated.

At the point of termination, we aren't sure if the former selling business will continue or whether the economic terms for using these ecommerce seller accounts will simply change. On the latter, we'd be unsure by how much.

Other than Kami, which is sharing revenue with AMCI, there is another related party which is Senslab, in which the CEO of AMCI has a 38% stake. This is where they get their security cameras from that they are currently selling:

During the years ended December 31, 2025 and 2024, 100% of inventories, were purchased from related parties, Senslab HK Limited and its subsidiary Senslab Technology Co., Ltd (collectively referred to as “Senslab”). Refer to Note 13 “Concentration Risk” for purchases from each supplier under Supplier Concentration. AMC Robotics Corporation has secured favorable payment terms with Senslab, extending the payment period from 60 days to 180 days

While it's nice that they have better terms, this is their major cash outflow and again its determined through related parties, and not arm's length. It's hard to know what to make of this, but perhaps it's not a long-term consideration if the security camera distribution activity is going to be phased out.

But if that's the case then where will the cash inflows be coming from? Supposedly, from the new Kami revenue sharing at 30% in perpetuity for as long as those subscribers stay subscribed. So those related party revenues, which should be capital lite, are supposed to sustain the business, we suppose. The incremental margin on that revenue must be quite high, so that could be a few more million entering into the cash flow picture, and also inventory spending falling out, together maybe bringing cash flow down to neutral levels - perhaps even net of greater R&D spend, something that we expect to occur.

Also, with commercializing a product like a logistics robot, we also expect some newer expenses

Bottom Line

In terms of dilution, there's not too much related to warrants associated to the PIPE financing. It's about a 10% effect, using the share count method. On a net new shares basis, it's probably more like 7-8%, so not very much.

But whether dilution will be needed in equity financings to supplement the $7 million in cash is unclear. At $5 million cash burn or so, the runway is a little less than 1.5 years. Q2 2026 is when the NovaArm is supposed to start bringing in money, so maybe there's not too much to worry about. Moreover, if they no longer sell their security cameras at some point this year, then they will be getting a solid $3 million+ inflow from that, without needing to spend on inventory which was the biggest cash outflow. Therefore, the cash needs should shrink, but again this is ignoring possibly significant R&D increase, where a decrease into this development year of 2025 seems unsustainable to us, and also other investments that may go into the newly announced Vietnam manufacturing footprint, which is for Kyro, and not for the soon to be released NovaArm. We are not sure what the cash draw should be for that product that is supposed to be released in Q2, but was in the advanced development phase lately.

In all, we're just not convinced we have a picture, and on the face of it there is so much related party stuff going on that it's very difficult to value the company's current operations, and it would always be difficult to value their products in the pipeline, when we don't really have any idea of how competitive and useful their offering will be in the market. It's hard to even say how capitally intense commercializing the NovaArm and eventually the Kyro will be relative to their cash balances. Not inviting a buy at the moment.

data.title: Palantir Has Become A Buy Ahead Of Earnings (Preview, Upgrade)
data.description:
Upgrading PLTR To "Buy" Ahead Of The Q1 Earnings Release

I've been bearish on Palantir Technologies (PLTR) for some time in the past, with my latest period of bearishness spanning from August 2025 to February 2026. In my February update, I saw that the sentiment had become too dark and that the stock had started to look more reasonably valued again after a valuation reset with no real changes in the core fundamentals. Since that latest update of mine, the stock has managed to soar by over 6%, slightly beating the market.

Now, Palantir is rapidly approaching its Q1 2026 earnings update that is expected to see the light on May 4th before the market opens. I'm not suggesting that the stock's valuation has fully reflected the new risk factors and the Anthropic (ANTHRO) threat - there might be more downside to come, for sure - but from what I see, PLTR looks like a strong pick on this prolonged dip right before earnings because the underlying business momentum is unlikely to have stalled yet. I believe management will provide several positive commentaries, and PLTR will, once again in its corporate history, report a massive Rule of 40, outshining most other tech firms out there. The risk/reward looks favorable ahead of the earnings print, so I decided to upgrade my rating to "Buy" for the first time since mid-2024.

Previewing Palantir's Upcoming Earnings Print

PLTR has lost over 20% on a YTD basis, which is a severe underperformance against the S&P 500 (SPY) and the tech sector (XLK) as well, although it seems to be just in line with the software sector's move (IGV):

The clearest catalyst for the sell-off was, of course, Anthropic's rise with their Claude Code and Mythos models, which have shown the market that they can execute complex workflows and autonomously coordinate tasks across multiple interfaces, thus potentially disrupting the whole SaaS world and its astronomical margins. The risk looks real to PLTR at first glance because Palantir is positioning far beyond the government space, where Anthropic's access got restricted (as of April 2026). The commercial end market might be at risk for Palantir if we imagine that Anthropic is indeed eating Palantir's lunch (the demand for PLTR's AIP offerings might be at risk).

Despite that risk, I think PLTR's commercial market's moat might be wider than most people think. It's about the technical details going first. Anthropic's Claude, like any other LLM, provides an infrastructure layer using a consumption-based token pricing model. For routine tasks and white-collar jobs, it might be a massive disruptive force if we assume that it requires adequate pricing. Palantir is operating at a slightly different angle, technically. Their moat is their ontology, which is basically a virtual layer that maps out all needed unstructured data silos into real-world business entities, creating a digital twin of the client's business process as a whole. Probabilistic LLMs tend to hallucinate, especially when they have a lot of input tokens for analysis, but Palantir provides a sort of "structural lock-in" that doesn't let their AI models operate outside the initially predefined business logic and access controls.

The strings of text produced by the language models are little without a software architecture that can lend grammar and structure to the output of these probabilistic prediction engines. The models must be tethered to objects in the real world, and it is that tether, that means of grounding and orientation, that we have built.

Source: Alex Karp's comments

From all that, I conclude that Claude will indeed be in demand for enterprise usage, but likely only for basic tasks that don't require deep integration with secure sandboxing. Palantir is very likely to serve its clients where it performs best - closer to high-stakes environments. It's a different layer of servicing demand, so to speak, at least the way I look at it.

Palantir's bootcamps and AIP platform have been driving the commercial revenues red hot in the previous quarters, thanks to the fact that they helped collapse enterprise sales cycles from 12-18 months down to just 30-90 days. In Q4 2025, U.S. commercial revenue grew by 137% YoY, and Palantir managed to close 180 deals worth at least $1 million, and 61 deals worth at least $10 million in Q4 alone, which is insanely strong. In the coming Q1, we need to see hard evidence, concrete numbers supporting the same dynamics, in order for the stock price action to calm down (and possibly reverse). Any deceleration from Q4 (or Q1 2025) will likely be punished. But what's important here - even if the commercial side's growth slightly matures, which is quite normal after a massive surge in Q4, the management will have its chance to speak about Anthropic's models. If they manage to convince the market that they're retaining their pricing power and winning enterprise budgets against the cheaper LLM alternative, and they decide to guide higher (for the consolidated sales or US commercial part alone), I'd expect a truly massive bullish catalyst for the stock in the medium term.

It's going to be a show-me quarter, and while the comments are the most important part, it's also nice to see what's currently priced in by the latest consensus data. The market expects to see $1.54 billion in consolidated net revenues (+74.27% YoY) and $0.28 in adjusted EPS (almost +115% YoY), according to Seeking Alpha. The management's guidance from Q4 was ~$1.534 billion for sales and ~$872 million in adjusted income from operations for Q4 (midpoints in both cases). Over the past 8 quarters, PLTR has easily exceeded both top and bottom lines, and that's why we usually saw a hefty premium on both headlines right before the earnings release date. This time, the premium is there, but it doesn't look as hefty as before to me. Yes, the earnings revisions look positive on the 3-6 months scale, but they're absolutely immaterial when we take a look at the past month's changes.

It tells me that the market has gotten cautious about what's coming next for PLTR and how it reports - the tendency we haven't seen since 2024 or so.

In terms of valuation, PLTR still looks pricey on most conventional metrics such as P/E, EV/EBITDA, and even forward P/S. But the growth longevity hasn't gone anywhere - it has only become better. The market expects from PLTR an 8-year EPS CAGR of ~20%, according to Seeking Alpha data, hence the valuation premium right away.

But if we assume PLTR beats its FY2027 EPS estimate by 10% - which hasn't been a problem for them in the past - and the market recognizes the Anthropic-Palantir distinction leading to an eventual return to 80–100x earnings, we could see PLTR trading at $185.13.:

$1.87 * 1.1 * 90x = $185.13/share (an upside potential of >31%)

Source: Oakoff's calculations

I'm not a fan of projecting such stretched multiples for my base-case scenarios, but PLTR is a bit of a different story - if its technology shows resilience in Q1, the stock’s valuation could make a real comeback to 100x+ in terms of P/E.

Given all the above points, I decided to upgrade my rating on PLTR to "Buy" ahead of the Q1 earnings release.

Risks

The biggest risk to my assumptions above lies in underestimating the threat from Claude. If you follow the tech news, you probably know that Anthropic constantly releases new products and models for a variety of corporate (and not only) needs. So, if at some point, they decide to target specifically what Palantir is doing, their technical ontology layer, and start to commercialize their know-how quickly, it will indeed pose a great risk to PLTR's forward growth rates. In this case, we won't be able to take 100x in earnings multiple seriously any longer. Assuming a cut of 15% to the FY2027 EPS projection, and also taking the 50x earnings as the new norm, I'm getting a price target of only ~$79.5/share. The downside in this bear base scenario would be over 43%, so please be cautious here.

Your Takeaway

The risks look high, but I still like PLTR ahead of its earnings release next week. I assume and expect that their US commercial bookings will be on the rise for the quarter, and that the management speaks positively regarding the firm's selling proposition and the ontology role for the firm's moat sustainability. To me, the Claude-related risks have become overblown, and I see PLTR trading 30% higher in the next 12-18 months. That's why I'm upgrading the stock before the Q1 updates.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Corning Sees Long-Term Tailwinds In Optics, Underwhelmed With Guidance
data.description:
Corning Incorporated (GLW) reported a robust start to FY26 with significant strength in the optics and solar businesses. With an announced multi-year, multibillion-dollar deal with Meta Platforms (META) announced to expand optics production capacity to outfit its multiple gigawatts of data center capacity under construction, along with two additional hyperscaler deals hinted at in the Q1 ’26 earnings call, Corning will be navigating the coming years in a position of strength, delivering substantial tailwinds for its optics business. Given the strong operational outlook, I am recommending GLW shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $203/share at 22.93x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Corning Incorporated Operational Update

Corning kicked off FY26 in a position of strength, expanding and extending its Springboard plan to an incremental $11b in annualized revenue generation by the end of 2028. Corning reorganized its business segments in Q1 ’26 to better align with its growth markets, adding solar, which includes solar, semiconductor polysilicon, wafer, and modules, and consolidated display and specialty materials into glass innovations.

Corning’s solar segment was a major growth driver in Q1 ’26, delivering 80% year-over-year growth as a standalone business unit. Despite the strong growth, solar may face some minor headwinds in the coming quarters as its wafer facility is running on temporary power and water systems due to the tight power market. In addition to this, the wafer facility is undergoing a plant turnaround as it gears up for a more permanent power solution along with production equipment upgrades to facilitate higher expected growth. Accordingly, Corning will be adding capacity to polysilicon, module, and wafer production. As part of its Springboard plan, Corning is targeting $2.5b in annualized revenue for the solar business by 2028, up from $1.2b in FY25. Resulting from the planned maintenance on the wafer facility, solar may face some margin compression in eq2’26 before ramping back up in Q3 ’26.

Solar could be a major growth market for Corning over the coming years despite the recent shift in tone towards renewable energy sources following the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. One example of a major development is Alphabet’s (GOOG) investment in expanding its renewable footprint in partnership with TotalEnergies (TTE) in a 15-year agreement for 1GW of solar power capacity. Alphabet has also acquired Intersect in 2025, a renewable energy developer, to bolster its renewable footprint.

The data center market may be fruitful for Corning over the coming years as construction picks up for multi-gigawatt facilities. Accordingly, the global data center footprint is expected to more than double by 2030 to 200GW of capacity, creating a substantial opportunity for Corning’s optical cabling business. McKinsey & Co. estimates that the global spend for data center capacity will reach $7T by 2030, spread across facilities, gray- & white-space infrastructure, and power infrastructure, amongst other infrastructure.

At the end of March 2026, Corning began constructing the expansion of its optical cable manufacturing capacity in collaboration with Meta Platforms (META) through a $6b agreement. The agreement was designed to accelerate Corning’s optical cable manufacturing capacity in order to support Meta’s ambitious data center growth plans, which are set to add roughly multiple gigawatts of compute capacity over the coming years.

For example, Meta announced plans to invest $10b in a Texas-based AI factory to add 1GW of capacity by 2028, another $10b in Indiana for a 1GW facility, and a 2GW facility in Louisiana, expected to be completed by 2030; the Louisiana facility has the ability to scale to 5GW in capacity over time.

It’s clear that Meta will likely be a large consumer of optical cables for intertwining communications systems across the massive server farms, creating a significant opportunity that can support Corning’s growth ambitions. Corning hinted in the Q1 ’26 earnings call that there are two additional hyperscalers with similar agreements for optics cabling, presenting a massive opportunity for growth over the coming decade.

In addition to these agreements, Corning extended its multi-year agreement with Lumen for data center interconnects and fiber-to-the-home, experiencing a strong recovery in Q1 ’26.

As for its specialty glass segment, growth is expected to experience headwinds throughout FY26 resulting from higher memory costs, potentially impacting consumer demand for electronics devices.

Corning Incorporated Financial Position & Forecast

Looking ahead to Q2 ’26, I’m forecasting Corning to generate $4.6b in net revenue with a core EPS of $0.78/share. The basis of my forecast includes the $30mm turnaround cost for the solar business in Q2 ’26 along with incremental growth in optics, delivering an improvement to margins in the previous year’s quarter. Looking through the duration of FY26, I’m forecasting substantial top-line growth driven by both solar and optics, driving higher margins for the remainder of the fiscal year through eFY28. Given the strong market opportunity amongst hyperscalers for delivering optical cables, I believe the growth trend may continue beyond 2030 as multi-gigawatt-scale data center sites are constructed and lit up.

Corning closed Q1 ’26 with $1.75b in cash & equivalents and $9b in debt on the balance sheet for a net leverage position of 1.55x net debt/aEBITDA. While leverage has expanded when compared to the previous year’s quarter, I believe it remains in a healthy range given Corning’s growth opportunity across solar and optics.

Risks Related to Corning Incorporated

Bull Case

Corning is expanding production capacity to service large-scale data center developments for optic cabling as well as expanding its footprint in solar power infrastructure. Optics growth will be backed and supported by multi-billion-dollar deals with its hyperscaler customers, notably Meta Platforms, to help scale capacity with more certainty for offtake. With large deals being struck to expand solar power capacity, Corning may realize strong tailwinds over the coming years.

Bear Case

Corning is heavily investing in expanding capacity over the coming years, potentially exposing itself to a decline if the data center growth story were to stall. With OpenAI (OPENAI) missing its revenue and user adoption targets, investors are questioning the AI growth story, which may add pressure to Corning in the near future.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

GLW shares are currently trading at a rich premium of 30.25x EV/aEBITDA, well above their historical 3-year trading range. With shares having experienced significant expansion to the premium in recent quarters, I suspect the market may be gaining optimism for data center infrastructure suppliers, potentially justifying a higher trading premium for GLW shares.

Fundamentally, I believe GLW shares should be priced at $203/share at 22.93x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA; I am recommending GLW shares with a Buy rating. Given the recent pullback in the share price post-q1’26 earnings, I suspect shares may gyrate lower over the coming months until more certainty is built into the growth strategy. The tone may change following Corning’s upcoming investor event on May 6, 2026, where management will be updating the targets for the Springboard program.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Boyd Gaming: Talking With The Bears
data.description:
Yesterday was one of those days.

Up early, decent breakfast, quick lift. Nothing out of the ordinary.

Then I opened my laptop and saw CBRE Group advising to ‘take profits’ on Boyd Gaming (BYD).

I immediately thought, 'Well, this deserves an article.'

As I already owe you one post-earnings, I'm going to do something similar to what I did with Golden Entertainment (GDEN) after it was downgraded by Truist.

And just to save you time before the debate, Boyd didn't exactly knock it out of the park this quarter.

If anything, it was a mediocre double miss.

While revenue came in at $997.4 million (a shortfall of only ~$3 million, practically in line), what stood out was the EPS of $1.60, almost 6.5% lower than the $1.71 that Street was targeting.

And if you want to find someone to blame, look to Nevada once more. More specifically, to Vegas.

Downtown being weak isn't new, but even Locals (where Boyd has strong assets like Sam's Town) started softening a few quarters ago.

And it's not just one headwind here, but two—both tied to Orleans.

One is Durango (if you want to know more about why Orleans is getting beaten by Durango in Locals, I recommend reading this article here) and the other is its own ongoing renovations that will only finish next year.

Suncoast (only ~9 miles from Orleans) is also being renovated and, if I'm not mistaken, it's also not at full capacity.

Nothing new here.

But hold your hats! It seems the Strip is outperforming Fremont this year.

While management said traffic in Fremont decreased by ~11% at the beginning of the year, Las Vegas (now facing much easier comparisons) even had a solid February.

Also, keep in mind that the LVCC just completed a $600 million renovation, which may have boosted trade show attendance (which, incidentally, saw a post-pandemic record for attendees).

What caught my eye is that hotels finally seem to have some breathing room on pricing. Year-to-date, ADR grew 5.3% with a slightly negative occupancy rate over the same period (-0.7%).

It's not ideal, of course.

Folks continue to complain that Las Vegas is extremely expensive, and you can see an influx of tourists to smaller cities like Reno. And, looking at the drop in GGR over the same period, you can see that the rollers have been going there.

Boyd in Vegas is much more focused on Locals than Downtown.

To give you an idea, they have 8 properties (if you consider the new Cadence Crossing) in Locals and only 3 in Downtown.

Could the drop in GGR hurt them more than Strip-heavy operators who rely more on Rooms and less on Gaming?

Well, to answer that I need to break down the quarterly results.

To kick things off, here's a peek at revenue by segment:

Here you can see that although Locals and Downtown reported revenue declines of approximately 2.5% and 4.1%, respectively, this was more than offset by the strong performance of the Midwest & South segment.

Pretty cool, right?

And perhaps the biggest advantage of this is that this segment has several properties in eight different states.

So it’s not like a dip in GGR from one state (say, Illinois) would really shake things up, this segment is pretty well protected.

I also like to take the time to look at the GGR of some states on this list.

Take Louisiana, for example. There, Boyd has ~5 properties riding double-digit GGR growth throughout FY 2025. Missouri (3 properties) also exploded above 25% in Q4 of last year alone.

Both above the low to mid-single-digit GGR growth for brick-and-mortar casinos nationally.

I think I've already shown you that they can more than compensate for weaker Locals.

But here's the catch.

I don't think Locals are structurally weak.

I agree that Orleans will need a full makeover to compete with Durango, but there are two things Street probably forgets:

  1. Many workers in Southern Nevada are employed in tipping jobs, and with the OBBB granting tax exemption on tips, Boyd projects approximately $40 million flowing into casinos annually.
  2. Taking away the impacts of a lower-capacity Orleans (which has its busiest area closed in Q1), EBITDAR would have been in line with Q1 FY 2025.

Speaking of EBITDAR, here’s how it looked in Q1:

I like to use EBITDA to make comparisons easier later on.

This time, EBITDA came in at $288.8 million, a margin of ~29% (slightly below my base case for the entire FY 2026) versus 31.2% in the same period last year.

But there's another catch here that you need to look at closely.

Corporate expenses skyrocketed by more than $7 million year-over-year, and if they had been similar, the EBITDA margin would have been 29.7%, much closer to my base case of 30%.

And there's more.

If you considered Orleans at 100% capacity, adjusting for the hit they took in EBITDA this year, I think the EBITDA margin would have been very close to or even higher than last year's.

Now that I've given you all the context of what happened this quarter, it's time to talk to the bears.

Talking With The Bears

I'll use CBRE's recent 'take profits' call on Boyd as a proxy for the bearish case making the rounds, and try to push back on it.

Not saying I’ve got it nailed or that CBRE is outright bearish, of course. But it’s a good window into how part of the Street is looking at the name right now.

From what I understand, their argument is based on the following points:

  1. Boyd reached the all-time high with the market already pricing in a good part of the story.
  2. The soft Q1 print pointed to some deeper cracks in Las Vegas, with the stock becoming more of an execution story than a re-rating.
  3. Midwest & South got a bit of a lift from friendlier weather versus last year.
  4. Without a short-term catalyst and targeting ~7x EBITDA for FY 2027.

In other words, they're saying between the lines that Boyd is a good company, but with a stretched valuation at this point.

Fair enough.

But I disagree on a few points here.

Firstly, I don't see Boyd delivering only a 15% price return here (CBRE tweaked the price target from $105 to $100), but ~25% up to my base case of $110 and at least a mid-single digit buyback yield along the way.

That is, something closer to 30% also counting the tiny dividend yield of ~0.9%.

Not bad, if you ask me.

Another point worth clearing up here is the catalyst story.

CBRE says the major catalysts would come post-FY 2028, but I respectfully disagree with them.

In just the next two years, Norfolk will likely open in late 2027, Orleans will begin and finish its renovation (giving Locals a facelift), Par-a-Dice will be relocated (some sources differ between 2027 and 2028 here), IP Biloxi will have renovated rooms, and there are some other projects.

And just so we don't forget, there's still the optionality of an M&A along the way.

As the CEO put it, Boyd is sitting on the strongest balance sheet it's ever had, and with the macro softening, that usually opens the door for picking up other operators.

The point I partially concede on is the perceived softness of Vegas.

Things are pretty soft there (especially Downtown, with all that Fremont exposure), but I'd argue the locals are holding up better than people give it credit for.

While Orleans hasn't been refurbished and Suncoast is projected to complete its run in Q3 FY 2026, it still has the tailwinds of the OBBB that should pick up as soon as the economy improves somewhat.

There's an upside here after the renovations before FY 2028 that I imagine is being overlooked.

A Separate Look at Valuation

I even broke this out in its own section.

I just don't buy a 'normalized' 7x EBITDA multiple for Boyd in FY 2027.

You've got a stack of catalysts just in that year plus the M&A angle with a clean balance sheet, and the view is still that it trades ~15% below peers at 8.3x? Boyd's own 5-year average of ~8x EBITDA already trades above.

Hard to square that.

But since they're anchoring on FY 2027, we might as well stick there too.

To start the discussion here, we can simplify things a bit and assume a mid-single-digit reduction in outstanding share per year (~$600 million annually).

In two years, Boyd would have ~69 million shares outstanding.

For revenue, Wall St. has a consensus of $4.23 billion. And it seems to me that they are not (correctly, of course) accounting for the impacts of Norfolk in the same year.

This time I will use the following EBITDA for each scenario:

  • Bear (27% EBITDA margin): $1.14 billion
  • Base (31% EBITDA margin): $1.31 billion
  • Bull (34% EBITDA margin): $1.43 billion

I also modeled net debt of ~$2 billion for FY 2026. Taking the base EBITDA, applying the same historical EBITDA-to-OCF (80%), I arrive at a projected OCF of $972 million.

FY 2027 Capex is an unknown.

I'll use something normalized close to ~$350 million. The FCF then comes in at $622 million.

Subtracting from that $600 million in buybacks and ~$60 million in dividends paid, we're talking about a cash deficit of just under $50 million.

For net debt in FY 2027, I will be pinpointing these $2.05 billion.

I won't use the normalized multiple of 7x for the reasons I've already stated. About 8.3x feels a lot more reasonable here.

Running that through the model, I land at $107.50 in the bear case, $128 in the base, and $142 on the bull.

But it's not that simple. I need to bring this to present value.

Using the $0.80 annual dividend that Boyd pays and plugging in growth in line with the CAGR since FY 2003 (~7.4%), the cost of equity here is 8.3%.

Discounting my price target to present value, the scenarios look like this:

  • Bear: $91.65
  • Base: $109
  • Bull: $121

Of course, I expect the base-case scenario.

For that to happen, just takes the market keeping Boyd around the industry multiple while the growth levers come through, and EBITDA margins settling about 300 bp above the 5-year average.

See, all of this is possible.

Higher corporate expenses are also one-off factors that shouldn't repeat in the coming quarters.

And it's important to make it clear that this valuation did not include an M&A model, which could have given this story another boost.

So for me, Boyd is still a long-term 'Buy.'

It's not the bargain it once was, but I still see a path to north of a 30% total return from here.

data.title: Middle East Turmoil Fuels Inflation Fears, Testing Fed's Patience
data.description:
The Federal Reserve is expected to keep its target interest rate unchanged in today’s policy announcement, but the stable outlook belies the unsettled inflation picture that’s keeping the bond market on edge.

Visibility for the rate outlook at today’s Fed meeting, by contrast, is clear as a bell. Fed funds futures are pricing in a 100% probability that the target rate will remain at its current 3.50%-3.75% range. After that, the clarity fades.

The bond market is struggling to price in the twin threats of higher inflation and slower economic growth—the dual effects of the Middle East turmoil that has elevated energy costs. The odds of a quick resolution remain low, a calculus reaffirmed after President Trump on Tuesday told aides “to prepare for an extended blockade of Iran, U.S. officials said,” according to The Wall Street Journal.

U.S. Treasury yields have increased recently but have pulled back from the peak that followed the start of the war on Feb. 28. The market is still flirting with the possibility of rate hikes, based on the policy‑sensitive 2‑year yield, which continues to trade above the effective Fed funds rate. That’s an indication that investor sentiment is pricing in modest odds that the central bank will be forced to raise interest rates at some point in the near term.

But not yet—and perhaps not for the next several FOMC meetings, or so Fed funds futures suggest.

The inflation bump has already begun and will likely persist in the near term. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) surged in March to a 3.3% year‑over‑year pace, up sharply from February’s 2.4%, driven by spiking energy costs. It’s unclear whether inflation will continue rising, but a safer bet is that it holds steady above 3% for now.

The Fed still has some leftover ammunition in its policy toolkit in the form of a mildly hawkish bias, based on a simple model that compares the target rate to unemployment and inflation—the two components of the central bank’s dual mandate. On that basis, policy is slightly tight. The question is whether that will suffice in the months ahead, as the inflationary effects of the war—and now stalemate—in the US–Iran conflict endure.

A complicating factor is that the blowback from Middle East turmoil will weigh on global economic activity, which will spill over into the U.S. to some degree. If output slows enough, that could offset the need to hike rates.

Exactly how the twin shocks of higher inflation and slower growth will play out remains uncertain, thanks to the fog of war (stalemate), which is why the bond market—and the Fed—are playing a wait‑and‑see game.

“On the dual mandate, they’d say we’re roughly at a stable labor market,” Roger Ferguson, an economist and former Fed vice chair, told CNBC. "On the inflation side of the mandate, [there’s] a lot more work to be done with a sticky 3% [inflation rate], and I hope they argue, 'we're going to sit tight for a little while to see how this all plays out.'"

Two real‑time proxies on my short list for monitoring these risks are the 10‑year Treasury yield and the price of crude oil. The 10‑year yield closed yesterday at 4.35%, still comfortably below the intraday peak of nearly 4.50% since the war’s start, but the benchmark rate is drifting higher again. As it moves closer to the previous peak—and certainly if it breaks above it—that will signal that the market is demanding a higher inflation premium, which in turn will put more pressure on the Fed to hike.

A similar calculus applies to crude oil, based on the US benchmark (West Texas Intermediate). The price closed yesterday above $103 a barrel. That’s still below the $120 peak set in the early days of the war, but prices are trending up again. If the market begins to test the upper range set during the war, that should be viewed as a sign that inflation pressures will persist, if not intensify, for longer than expected.

In the end, the Fed may be standing still, but the markets certainly aren’t. With inflation simmering and geopolitical tensions refusing to fade, investors are left navigating a landscape where every data point feels like a potential turning point. The calm of today’s decision may not last long.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Apple: Tim Cook Exits At The Top
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) heads into the seasonally slow period with Tim Cook set to exit the CEO position now. The tech giant is not in a particularly strong operational position with constant failures on implementing AI. My investment thesis remains ultra Bearish on the stock as the iPhone replacement cycle ends and the future sales catalyst is unknown.

Pent Up Demand

Apple is set to report FQ2'26 earnings on Thursday, April 30, with the consensus analyst estimates as follows:

  • EPS of $1.94 per share, up 17.9% YoY
  • Revenue of $109.73 billion, up 15.1% YoY

The tech giant is set to follow up the solid FQ1 report with another strong quarter. Apple had gone 4 years without quarterly revenue growth topping 10%.

Apple reported a surprise FQ1 revenue beat topping $5 billion due to apparent pent-up-demand for the iPhone. The company reported iPhone revenue surge 23% YoY to $85.3 billion, coming off a 4-year period where sales growth regularly were near the flatline.

The company has already apparently lost the foldable iPhone as a catalyst. DigiTimes suggests the foldable phone has been delayed by a couple of months to at least August, but other reports have the iPhone Fold slipping into 2027.

The product is slated to cost $2,000 to $2,500 and likely will face initial product shortages. Apple struggled selling the Apple Vision Pro with the product having a $3,500 starting price, questioning how many consumers want a product costing double the typical iPhone.

The pent-up-demand cycle fits perfectly with IDC forecasts for iPhone sales to slip this year. As highlighted in prior research, Apple increasing faces issues with smart glasses from Meta Platforms (META) and other vendors.

The memory chip shortage should contribute to a far tougher market ahead with IDC now predicting a dramatic 13% decline in global smartphone sales. The forecast is for just a slight revenue decline due to better results for the large vendors like Apple with only a modest rebound in 2027.

CEO Exit

Tim Cook is set to move to the Executive Chairman position on September 1 after being at the helm since replacing legendary co-founder Steve Jobs on August 24, 2011. John Ternus is taking over the CEO position following his current role as Senior Vice president of hardware engineering.

Mr. Cook has had an exceptional career at Apple, but his biggest success was improving the valuation multiple investors pay for the stock. Apple regularly traded at 10x forward EPS targets and the stock now trades at over 31x FY26 EPS targets.

The stock is up nearly 1,900% since Tim Cook took over while revenues haven't even grown 300%. Mr. Ternus faces a difficult period not so dissimilar to the scenario when Steve Ballmer took over at Microsoft (MSFT) following Bill Gates.

After the tech bubble, Microsoft famously went through a period from 2000 to 2014 where the company grew the business, but the stock was flat to down the whole 14-year period. The market wasn't convinced Mr. Ballmer had the tech giant on the right path and Microsoft saw the valuation shrink his whole tenure.

The ironic part is that both the Tim Cook era at Apple and the Steve Ballmer era at Microsoft lasted close to 15 years with very similar revenue growth during the period of 200% to 300%. The stocks fared far different with Tim Cook generating 1,900% gains while Ballmer saw the stock fall during this period.

The new Apple CEO will enter the position with Apple in a questionable AI strategy. The company has to use Gemini from Google (GOOG) to build a foundational model to fully get Siri and Apple Intelligence off the ground later this year while other tech giants are full-speed ahead building AI services.

Apple has already seen crucial App Store revenue growth slip to only ~5% in FQ1 with the U.S. market essentially flat. If the tech giant was seeing any real benefit, the company would see subscriptions for AI services lead to a boom in growth.

Whether due to pent-up-demand trailing off after FQ2, memory prices impacting iPhone sales, or a delay in the iPhone Fold, Apple is forecast to watch sales slip back to minimal mid-single digit growth rates for the next few years through at least FY28.

Unless Apple changes this scenario, the stock is outrageously priced with a new CEO taking over the helm and ongoing product issues with both AI and the newest hardware product in the iPhone Fold. The stock trades at 31x the FY26 EPS of $8.51 while a more logical valuation for the above growth is only a 15x multiple, or $128.

The Steve Ballmer scenario is on the table for the new Apple CEO. Any lack of execution will leave investors on the sidelines for an extended period while the company could continue growing at the 5% rate predicted by analysts.

Naturally, Apple has a long history now of trading at elevated levels. The stock could rise, as valuation is never a limiting factor. The company could even finally execute on an AI strategy with the new CEO figuring out a way for the company to monetize future AI uses on Apple devices.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Apple has oddly held near all-time highs despite long-time CEO with a surprise exit and the business headed to a difficult few years with slowing iPhone demand and the lack of a monetizable AI product. Tim Cook appears to be exiting just as the company has produced a couple of solid quarters.

Investors should use any pop on a strong FQ2 post-earnings rally as an opportunity to cash out at a premium valuation while risks mount under a new CEO.

data.title: A Bear Market Is Due: I'm Buying This Industry Anyway
data.description:
Lately, I’ve been watching a ton of interviews with “expert” economists and reading the bears on the broader markets (both articles and comments) here on Seeking Alpha.

One pattern keeps showing up. I see a certain level of frustration that the markets are not behaving the way they "should".

I mean, consumer sentiment is at all time lows, gas prices in the U.S. are still over $4/gallon, the CME Group survey is now showing December 2027 as the earliest date for a rate cut, Hormuz is still blocked with the US turning back Iranian tankers, and there are no early signs of a peace deal.

Despite all that, the SPY is barely $5 below the ATHs as I'm writing this piece. The music is still playing, and in my view, despite the near-term uncertainty, it’s likely to get louder as the year goes on.

However, I don't think the tide will lift all the boats equally. In fact, I am hyperselective with the pockets of the market that I've been buying over the last few weeks. More than ever, I think we are in a stock-picker’s market.

In this piece, I discuss my favourite industry: semiconductors. That said, this analysis largely stays at the industry level, and I don't cover the bull case behind the specific stocks that I own in this sector (otherwise, this would be a 10k-word article).

I also discuss some of the risks that are worth considering; some of them keep me up at night.

Before I start, a quick side note for readers new to my investing style.

My Investing Style

My investment style is anything but noble. I follow the rhythm of the market, which sophisticated investors often associate with "following the crowd."

Why not pick through the wreckage (i.e., stocks down 90%+ from their ATHs) in foreign markets or unloved industries (think specialty chemicals), where there is clear mispricing for anyone willing to open a balance sheet or cash flow statement?

Time.

I'm not patient enough to see a position in the red for multiple years until the market recognizes its value. Especially not while a clear bull market is unfolding. The cost of opportunity in locking in my capital in deep value plays for years until I see returns (if at all) is way too high in the current AI supercycle.

So, what tune is the market dancing to right now?

Follow Where Hyperscaler CapEx Is Going

The chart below represents the returns of the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOXX) vs the S&P 500 in the last decade.

Notice the S&P 500 is essentially a flat line in comparison, despite returning 247% in that period.

To understand why, take a look at the chart below representing the CapEx forecast for 2026 (as of February this year) vs the last few years:

To put $646B into perspective, take a look at the chart below representing G7 military spending vs hyperscaler capex:

Ok, fine. Nice figures. How much of that CapEx ends up in the semiconductor industry?

Intuitively, I knew it was "a lot", but I couldn't quantify it until Microsoft's Q1 2026 earnings call, when CFO Amy Hood (finally) made the following remark:

Capital expenditures were $37.5 billion, and this quarter, roughly two thirds of our capex was on short-lived assets, primarily GPUs and CPUs.

There you go: the answer is 66%. To me, that's a good enough rough estimate.

Other analysts have recently estimated that semis (GPUs, racks, memory, storage, etc.) account for about 70% of spending on a 10MW data center.

I cannot overstate how important the chart above is. Therefore, it shouldn’t surprise you that my two favorite industries right now are semis and power infrastructure. Let’s start with the first one.

The Semiconductor Industry Bull Case

Let me start with a "fun fact": global semiconductor sales have already moved into a $1 trillion annualized regime.

The latest hard data from the SIA (Semiconductor Industry Association) is showing global semiconductor sales of $88.8 billion in February 2026, up 7.6% month over month and up 61.8% year over year.

If you annualize the February $88.8 billion monthly sales figure, you get approximately $1T in sales.

Looking at the latest results of the largest semiconductor foundry in the world, TSMC (a topic I discussed in this piece), the company reported (in U.S. dollars) Q1 2026 revenue of $35.90 billion, up 40.6% yoy and 6.4% sequentially. Notably, both net income and diluted EPS increased 58.3% yoy, meaning that earnings growth exceeded revenue growth by 23.2 percentage points.

On the guidance, the company guided a Q2 revenue midpoint of $39.6 billion, which implies a sequential growth of about 10.3%. On gross margins, the Q2 GM midpoint of 66.5% is slightly above Q1’s 66.2%. So, we have higher revenue without margin dilution.

The above is "good enough proof" for me to assume that the peak of the AI supercycle (call it a bubble) is not yet in sight.

And that's just the tip of the iceberg. If I consider other giants in the semi space like Nvidia, the company reported Q4 FY2026 revenue of $68.1 billion, up 20% qoq and 73% yoy. On top of that, at NVIDIA GTC 2026, Jensen Huang said NVIDIA now sees at least $1 trillion in revenue from 2025 through 2027.

By the way, this was an increase from the $500 billion Blackwell/Rubin revenue opportunity through 2026 that Jensen had cited in the last earnings call. So, the new GTC figure represents a 2x increase in the visible opportunity, with the horizon extended by just one year.

On the memory side, it turns out that memory chips are the bottleneck for AI training, inference, and datacenter storage. Last week, SK Hynix said that customer requests for HBM supply over the next three years already “far exceed” production capacity.

For reference, TredForce has recently published its forecast for Q2 this year, and the DRAM and NAND flash prices are still showing a strong increase.

This pricing power is clearly visible in the margin story of these players. Back in March, Micron reported Q1 non-GAAP gross margin rising to 74.9% from 56.8% a quarter ago and 37.9% a year earlier.

On the equipment side, ASML raised earlier this month its 2026 net sales guidance to €36B - €40B with a gross margin expected between 51% - 53%. Other companies like SEMI are now projecting 300mm fab equipment spending to rise 18% to $133B in 2026 and 14% to $151B in 2027.

How about valuations? Well, despite the parabolic chart below, Micron is still trading at 9x forward earnings.

Of course, there are expensive pockets within semiconductors, like for example AMD, which is trading at 50x forward earnings. That said, the premium is mostly due to the optionality to take over market share from Nvidia. In fact, that's why Nvidia is trading at just 26x forward earnings.

I'll move now to the risks, as not everything is rainbows and sunshine in this industry.

Risk To The Semiconductor Industry

So, you're essentially timing the peak of the supercycle, right? (some may ask)

Yes, you got it right. What I’m doing here is basically betting I can get out before the AI bubble pops. In fact, when I see the first cockroaches, I will likely short the same players that I'm riding right now on the way up.

To understand why I believe timing the peak of the bubble is possible, I wrote a piece last year that I highly recommend as a read:

Aside from timing the peak of the AI supercycle, there are other (bigger) risks to consider.

Right now, there is a lot of uncertainty around China regarding semiconductor equipment exports. Specifically, the proposed U.S. MATCH Act could block ASML’s DUV tool sales and servicing in China. Some analysts believe the new rules could lead to a possible EPS reduction of up to 10%.

If that proposal goes through, I think the broader semiconductor equipment industry (think Lam Research, Applied Materials, KLA, Tokyo Electron) will go down. By how much and how long is impossible to say.

However, given the 6-month returns of the constituents seen in the chart below, I wouldn't be surprised to see a double-digit correction in this industry.

For disclosure, I own LRCX and AMAT as of this writing, so this risk is front and center for me.

Of course, there are more general risks, like a slowdown in hyperscaler CapEx spending due to overcapacity. Overall, I think this is what will pop the AI bubble. That said, I will only worry about this risk when hyperscalers use the word "digestion" during the earnings calls.

On the memory side, the likes of Micron, SK Hynix, and Samsung have benefited from the pricing hike in memory chip prices due to supply constraints. So far, analysts are projecting DRAM prices up 125% and NAND prices up 234% in 2026, with meaningful relief not expected until late 2027.

When that relief happens, I expect a sharp selloff in these names, as they will lose their earnings power.

Lastly, outside of the AI demand, the auto, industrial, PC, and smartphone markets are anything but exciting. For example, earlier this year, Onsemi missed Q4 revenue, with revenue down 11% and segment declines up to 17%, due to an inventory glut.

In the auto world, global EV registrations fell 11% in February 2026, with China down 32% yoy.

On the PC and smartphone side, PC and smartphone shipments are expected to decline in 2026 due to memory cost inflation (precisely, the same reason why the memory stocks are up right up).

Lastly, one shouldn't forget the bottlenecks. According to some analysts, nearly 40% of U.S. data center projects from the likes of Microsoft, Oracle, and OpenAI that are expected to open in 2026 are at risk of being delayed by at least three months.

So far, none of these players has announced any delays. Heading into the Q1 2026 earnings print, I think such an announcement could be a big downside risk for semiconductor stocks. Why?

Because it may delay orders, pushing the revenue and earnings estimates to the right.

Conclusion

Yes, we are in a bubble. No, I don't think the burst is in sight.

The reason I think we are so in a bubble is that hyperscaler CapEx is way ahead of the upside that AI brings to their businesses. For example, Amazon's CEO Andy Jassy disclosed for the first time the AWS AI revenue.

For reference, the AWS run rate at the start of the year was about $142B. How much do you think of that was driven by AI services?

Half? 75%? 90%?

Well, far from that. The figure is less than 10%. Specifically, AWS’s AI revenue run rate was over $15 billion in Q1 2026. Yes, that's nearly 260x larger than AWS at the same point. However, I cannot help but wonder if burning $200B in CapEx this year justifies $15B in AI revenue.

Overall, I have little to no doubts that we will see a major correction in the markets. However, betting too early on it makes no sense to me. First, you are losing upside from the bull run. Second, you are not entering your short at a favorable price, which means that, even if the correction happens, you capitalize only a small part of it.

Bottom line: I love semis. I own semis. I’ll dance with semis as long as the market keeps playing the music. That said, at the first sign that the music (i.e., hyperscaler CapEx) is slowing down, I’m out.
